Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/A.title: Application Layer
Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/A.content:
Application Layer
World Wide Web
WWW is based on the idea of hypertext and hyperlinks. It was extremely successful due to the following factors:

simplicity of HTML
simplicity of HTTP
low entry barrier for publishers
GUI browsers (e.g. Mosaic, Netescape)

An end system called a host may run multiple programs which run multiple processes. A process is addressed within its host by a port number.
Applications use the network through sockets, which is an interface provided by the operating system.
Architecture of a typical network communication:

Client application

Create a socket  by connecting to host  on port .
Read and write data using the socket .
Disconnect by destroying .


Server application

Create a socket  by accepting a connection on host  on port .
Read and write data using the socket .
Disconnect by destroy .



Some applications have processes that act both as clients and servers, often called Peer-to-Peer (P2P) architecture.
Hyper-Text Transfer Protocol
The main purpose of HTTP is to provide access to Web objects through a connection-oriented transport mechanism.

Consists of a sequence of requests issued by the client.
Responses issued by the server, each in response to a single request.
Stateless protocol.

HTTP runs on port 80 with TCP.
Example of an HTTP request:
GET /~kgk/212/index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.doc.ic.ac.uk
User-agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept-Language: en-GB

Host is passed as a parameter so that server can host multiple virtual sites e.g. Dokku
Example of an HTTP response:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Thu, 26 Jan 2017 16:00:00 GMT
Server: Apache
Last-Modified: Sat, 21 Jan 2017 20:05:55 GMT
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Content-Length: 5470
Content-Type: text/html
...
<!DOCTYPE html>
...

Telnet allows us to send plain-text commands directly to a server listening on a specific port.
Protocol Version of HTTP
A HTTP protocol should always include a version number.
The first version of HTTP used one TCP connection per object, which is both an inefficient use of the network and the operating system.
HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections, in which multiple requests and responses can be issued. Connection: close in the request and response asks the server/client to close the persistent connection.
HTTP/2 is expected to completely replace HTTP/1.x within the next few years. In HTTP/2, content is binary, connection is not ordered/blocking and thus capable of using a single (TCP) connection for parallelism.
HTTP Methods

GET: retrieves the object at the URL.
POST: submits the enclosed data to the server.

Not idempotent e.g. x = x + 1.


HEAD: only retrieves the response header of the object at the URL.
PUT: requests the enclosed data to be stored under the URL.

Idempotent e.g. x = 4.


DELETE: requests the object at the URL to be deleted.
OPTIONS: requests the available communication options for the object at the URL.

HTTP Headers

Object characterisation

e.g. Content-Type, Content-Length, Content-Encoding


Content negotiation

e.g. Accept-Charset, Accept-Encoding


Cache management

e.g. Expires, Last-Modified, ETag


Explicit cache control

e.g. Cache-Control


Method-specific responses

e.g. Allow


Authorisation/identification

e.g. Authorization



HTTP Status Codes

1xx: Informational
2xx: Successful operation
3xx: Redirection

Moved permanently (301)
Found/Moved temporarily (302)


4xx: Client error

Malformed request (400)
Unauthorised (401)
Object not found (404)
Method not allowed (405)


5xx: Server error

Internal server error (500)
Service overloaded (503)



Structure of HTTP Request

Request line

Method (e.g. GET)
Space
URL (e.g. /~kgk/212/index.html)
Space
Protocol version (e.g. HTTP/1.1)


Header lines (optional)

Header name (e.g. Host)
Colon
Space
Header value (e.g. www.doc.ic.ac.uk)


Empty line
Object body (optional)
Line terminator (CRLF)

Structure of HTTP Response

Status line

Protocol version (e.g. HTTP/1.1)
Space
Status code (e.g. 405)
Space
Status message (e.g. Method Not Allowed)


Header lines (optional)

Header name (e.g. Date)
Colon
Space
Header value (e.g. Thu, 26 Jan 2017 16:00:00 GMT)


Empty line
Object body (optional)
Line terminator (CRLF)

CR (\r) and LF (\n) both required for Windows and HTTP.
Only LF (\n) required for Unix.



Web Caching
With web caching, a client request goes to a proxy/cache server.
The proxy may:

Forward the request to the origin server, acting as a client.
Retrieve the response from the origin server.
Cache the object for some time.
Forward the response back to the client.

The benefits of web caching include:

Reduced latency
Reduced network traffic
Privacy
Protection from outside access

Cache Control for Web Caching

HEAD request is used to obtain cache information about the object.

e.g. to see if object has been updated.


Servers specify explicit expiration times in HTTP headers.
Cache-Control: no-cache

Request: proxy should not return the cache.
Response: client should not cache this object


Cache-Control: max-age=X

Request: proxy should return the cache if less than X seconds old.
Response: client should keep the cache up to X seconds.

Should be used with must-revalidate otherwise the restriction is optional.




Expires: X

Request: proxy should return the cache if before X.
Response: client should keep the cache until X.

Should be used with must-revalidate otherwise the restriction is optional.




If-Modified-Since: X

Request: proxy should only return the cache if modified since X.



Sessions and Cookies
HTTP provides Cookies as the means for higher-level applications to maintain stateful sessions.

Request: Cookie header is used to tell the server that the request belongs to the given session.
Response: Set-Cookie header is used to tell the client to store the given cookie as a session identifier.

Dynamic Webpages

Common Gateway Interface (CGI)

URL specifies the program and its parameters.
Server starts a process to execute the program and return response.
Stateless.


Servlets

Java-based solution for CGI
Stateful.


Embedded scripts

Server-side: PHP
Client-side: JavaScript



IP Addresses and Host Names
An end system is identified and addressed by its IP address.

32 bits in IPv4
128 bits in IPv6

An IP address is easy to process for computers but are not practical for everyday use. Host names are used for this purpose.
The mapping from host names to ip addresses are resolved by:

/etc/hosts file

User-defined host names.
Used up to 1983 when there were not many hosts.


Domain Name System (DNS)

Distributed lookup facility.



Domain Name System
Primary function of the DNS is to map a name to an IP address.
The name space was divided into non-overlapping zones, each of which is handled by one or more name servers.

Root servers

Holds the map from the top-level domain to the associated NS.


Top-level domain servers

Holds the map from the second-level domain to the associated NS.


Authoritative server

Holds the map of public hosts within the given domain.



DNS runs on top of UDP because it can simply resend the request if no response is received.
Resource Records of DNS
DNS is essentially a database that contains Resource Records (RR) with the following columns:
There are several values available for Type column in RR:

Type A

Maps a host name to its IP address.
Name is the host name.
Value is the IP address.


Type NS

Maps a host name to its name server.
NS holds Type A/NS record for the host name.
Name is the host name.
Value is the NS host name.


Type CNAME

Maps a host name to its canonical host name.
Name is the host name (alias).
Value is the canonical host name.
e.g. www3.imperial.ac.uk to www.imperial.ac.uk


Type MX

Maps a host name to its mail exchange server .
Name is the host name.
Value is the host name for the MX server that handles mail for the given host name.



TTL column specifies the time to live, which tells how long the record should be valid for.
Round Robin DNS
Round robin DNS is a technique for balancing the load of geographically-distributed Web servers.

DNS server holds a mapping from host name to multiple name servers, one of which is returned in round robin order per request.
TTL should be small because these may constantly change depending on server load.

DNS Caching
The benefits of DNS caching include:


Improved performance


Reduced load on the DNS infrastructure


/etc/resolv.conf file contains a list of DNS cache servers

Typically only contains the router’s IP address.
The router queries the local ISP’s DNS cache server.
The local IPS’s DNS cache server returns the DNS cache, or queries the authoritative NS (e.g. root, top-level).



DNS cache poisoning is a type of cyber attack that exploits a vulnerability in the DNS to redirect users to fraudulent websites. This can be prevented by the authoritative NS signing RR etc.
Content Distribution Networks
Streaming content to a large number users of simultaneously:

Single large server

Single point of failure.
Point of network congestion.
Long path to distance users.


Content distributed networks

Stores multiple copies of videos at multiple geographically distributed sites.



CDN cluster design strategies:

Enter deep strategy

Push CDN servers deep into many access networks.
10-100K servers.


Bring home strategy

Smaller number of larger clusters in PoPs near access networks.
10-100 servers.



CDN cluster selection strategies:

Pick a CDN node geographically closest to client.
Pick a CDN node with shortest delay to client.

CDN nodes periodically ping local ISPs and report results to CDN DNS server.



CDN DNS server only knows the local DNS cache server, which may not be fully accurate:

Public DNS

When DNS cache is unavailable, queries CDN DNS server with the client’s actual IP.

Performs better redirection.


e.g. 8.8.8.8 by Google, 1.1.1.1 by CloudFlare.


Client approach

By Netflix
CDN DNS servers return a list of several CDN servers.
Client pings and picks the best server based on RTT.



Electronic Mail
Features and goals of emails:

Asynchronous communication
One-to-many communication
Multimedia content

Architecture of email systems:

User agent

Allows a user to read and write emails.


Mail transfer agent (mail sever)

Accept messages for remote delivery .

Store messages in a local persistent queue.
Deliver messages to the destination server using the transport protocol.


Accept messages for local delivery.

Save messages in some local persistent mailbox.
Allow user agents to access local mailboxes using the access protocol.





Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
SMTP runs on port 25 with TCP.
The standard message format has some serious limitations:

No encryption.

SMTPS adds encryption by enabling TLS.


Only ASCII characters.
Only supports text format.

Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions (MIME) specification defines useful extensions to SMTP:

text/plain: normal ASCII message.
text/html: HTML formatted message.
image/jpeg:  contains only a JPEG image.
multipart/mixed: contains multiple media parts.

Example of SMTP message:
HELO example.com
MAIL FROM: imperialcollege@example.com
RCPT TO: email@ic.ac.uk
DATA From: Imperial College <imperialcollege@example.com>
To: Test User <email@ic.ac.uk>
Subject: This is the subject!

This is the body!
.
QUIT

SMTP Headers

To: e-mail address(es) main destination
Cc: e-mail address(es) to send copies
Bcc: e-mail address(es) to send blind copies
From: name of sender(s)
Sender: e-mail address sender
Received: line added by each intermediate transfer agent
Return-path: return address
Date: the date and time the message was sent
Subject: short summary of the message
Reply-To: email address to which replies should be sent

The Sender field is often the same as From and Sender so can be left out.
Structure of SMTP Message

HELO line

HELO
Space
Host name


Header lines (optional)

Header name
Space
Header value


Empty line
Message body
A single dot .
QUIT line

SMTP client checks if a line of message body starts with a dot, and inserts another dot in front. SMTP server checks if a line of message body starts with a dot and another character, and deletes the first dot.
Post Office Protocol
SMTP assumes that the user agent and transfer agent run on the same host. POP3 allows a user to access the host mailbox from a different machine.
POP3 runs on port 110 with TCP.
Example of POP3 message:
+OK POP3 server ready
USER kgk
+OK
PASS longcatislong
+OK login successful
LIST
1 2505
.
RETR 1 (pops/sends message #1)
DELE 1
QUIT
+OK POP3 server disconnecting

Internet Message Access Protocol
POP3 deletes retrieves message from the host by default. IMAP stores mail on the host server so that it can be accessed by multiple machines and users.
File Transfer
File Transfer Protocol
www.jscape.com/blog/active-v-s-passive-ftp-simplified
FTP enables the exchange of files between a client and a server.

Uses two connections to transfer files:

Control connection is used to send commands and responses.
Data connection is used to transfer the actual files.


Enables more reliable and efficient data transfer


Active mode

Client establishes the control connection.
Server establishes the data connection (i.e. server takes active role).

Client port is random, which may be blocked by firewall.




Passive mode

Client establishes the control connection.
Client establishes the data connection (i.e. server takes passive role).

Client port is still random, but firewalls usually allow inbound traffic from sessions initiated by the client.





Other Application Layer Protocols

Secure Shell (SSH)

Direct encrypted communication.


Telnet

Plain text direct communication.


Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP)

Enables a system administrator to manager network devices.


Network File System (NFS)

Enables file access over the network.


Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)

Allows devices to receive an IP address within the network.

Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/D.title: Data Link Layer
Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/D.content:
Data Link Layer
WiFi
Also known as IEEE 802.11.
Wireless access point uses 2.4GHz or 5GHz of the radio spectrum, which are open for unlicensed use. It merely work as a wireless repeater.
Bands

ISM band

Reserved for industrial, scientific and medical purposes.


U-NII band

Known as unlicensed national information infrastructure.



These bands can be used by unlicensed purposes and can be prone to interference by:

Cordless phones
Baby monitors
Bluetooth
NFC
Microwave ovens
Amateur radio, etc.

Ethernet
Also known as IEEE 802.3.
Cable Types

UTP (unshielded twisted pair)
STP (shielded twisted pair; braid screen around cables)
FTP (foil twisted pair; foil shield around cables)
SFTP (shielded and foil twisted pair)

Twisting reduces the strength of noise signals, and shielding ethernet cables prevents eavesdropping through EMI.
UTP cables were cheap because they were used in telephone systems with mass production.
Cable Pinouts
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Differential_signalling
In Ethernet, there are two pairs of wires used for differential signalling, which means that the data signal is transmitted as a pair of electrical signals with opposite polarities:

TX+ and TX-: used for transmitting data
RX+ and RX-: used for receiving data

The receiver then looks at the difference between the two signals to interpret the data. Some benefits of differential signalling include double signalling voltage, reduced noise and low power consumption.
Cable Flavours
www.computercablestore.com/straight-through-crossover-and-rollover-wiring
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rollover_cable

Straight-through: for communication between different layers (e.g. switch to router)
Crossover: for communication between the same layer (e.g. switch to switch)
Rollover: for directly tapping into a networking device (e.g. troubleshoot a router from the console port)

A straight-through cable connects TX+/- to TX+/- and RX+/- to RX+/-. A crossover cable connects TX+ to RX+, TX- to RX- and vice versa. A rollover cable has reversed wiring which connects a pc to a router’s serial communication port (using a different standard).
A PC or router uses the MDI specification, while a switch uses MDI-X specification, which comes with a crossover inside it. This is why we need to use a straight-through for connecting switch to pc, and a crossover cable for switch to switch/pc to pc. Check this link for the motivation behind the crossover.
Some modern devices know how to fake-swap between a straight and a crossover using software based approach (Auto-MDI/MDIX).
Frames
An Ethernet frame:

Provides well-defined interface to Network Layer for sending/receiving packets.
Identifies transmission errors using a Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) checksum.

The CRC checksum is stored in the footer.
Mac Address
Every NIC conforming to IEEE 802 has a MAC address:

48 bits written as 6 colon-separated octets in hexadecimal.
OUID (first 3 octets) is manufacturer specific

Guarantees uniqueness (c.f. ICANN and ISP for IP addresses).


I/G bit (last bit in first octet) decides if unicast or multicast address.

Unicast means the address is individual
Multicast means it is a group address.


U/L bit (second last bit in first octet) decides if globally or locally administered.

MAC address can be assigned to a PC, router or switch.
A host MAC address is a unicast and globally administered address, while a broadcast address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) is a multicast and local administered address.
Repeater, Hub, Switch and Router
Repeaters, hubs, switches and routers have different purposes and operate in different OSI layers:

Repeater

Repeats data in LAN.
Boosts the signal and extends the range of network.
Receives and forwards data from all ports.
Wireless version is known as wireless access point (WAP).
Physical layer because it only handles signalling.


Hub

Connects devices in LAN.
Broadcasts data to all connected devices.
Physical layer because it only handles signalling.


Switch

Connects devices in LAN.
Sends data to the destination device only.
Reduces the network congestion.
Data-link layer because it needs to read the MAC address in a frame.


Bridge

Switch with only two ports.
Only sends frames connection if the MAC address of the destination matches.


Router

Connects multiple LANs.
Sends data to the destination network.
Network layer because it needs to read the IP address in a packet.



A switch uses the FIB MAC table to remember which port is connected to which device by its MAC address.
A router comes with many other features, such as firewalls, NAT and DHCP server.
Switching Methods

Store-and-forward

Receives the whole frame and forwards it.
Slow for large frames.
Switch can check the checksum on behalf.


Cut-through

Start forwarding when you know the header.
Fast even for large frames.
Recipient must check the checksum.



Switching Loops
A switching loop occurs when there is more than one layer 2 paths between two endpoints (e.g. multiple connections between two switches or two ports on the same switch connected to each other).
Use the following protocols to ensure there are no loops:

Spanning Tree Protocol
Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol

Network Topology

Switched Ethernet Topology

Also known as star topology.
Single machine connected to each switch port.
Sniffing is usually impossible.


Inter-networking Ethernet Topology

Repeater and switches.
Some machines are directly connected to each other via cables.


Wireless Network Topology

Wireless access points.
Sniffing is easy.



Inter-networking Ethernet Topology
Inter-networking Ethernet Topology can be further categorised:

Bus Topology

BNC T connector to add a new host.
BNC terminator to mark the end of network.


Ring Topology

Two NICs required per host (expensive).
Data flow around the ring.
A cut in link kills the entire network.


Dual Ring Topology

Four NICs required per host (more expensive).
Faster data flow with backup link.


Token Ring Topology

Connects all hosts in a logical ring (not necessarily physical).
MSAU controlled the mapping from the logical ring to physical network.
Frames are passed around the logical ring.
Frame are removed from network when reaching the sender.



Token Ring Topology
Token Ring Topology has listen mode and transmit mode:

Listen mode

Copies frame into buffer.
Re-transmits frame to next host.
Host can read the buffer at any time.


Transmit mode

Transmits frames from memory.
Drains frames arriving back.
Only one host can be in transmit mode.

To reduce frame collisions.


May release the token before draining all frames (early release).



When there is no data to send, a special frame, called a token, is passed around the circle indefinitely.
A token consists of (pre-defined) start/end delimiter and access control flags. A frame additionally contains the data to be sent, frame control, source/destination MAC address and frame status.
Schemes of Token Ring Topology

Priority scheme

Host can claim the token if the priority of host is as high as the priority of data.


Reservation scheme

Host can reserve the right to transmit high-priority data.
Host wanting to transmit data can set reservation priority in the current frame (not token).
Host transmitting frames sees the frame with reservation priority.

When transmission is complete, sends a token with this priority so that other hosts cannot claim the token unless they have higher-priority data.




Acknowledgement scheme

Frame status A: destination is currently busy.
Frame status C: destination correctly read the frame.



Complexities of Token Ring Topology

Assumes no one abuses the priority/reservation scheme.
Active monitor station is required to:

Re-generate lost token.
Drain orphan frames.


Any host can become active monitor station.

Elect new monitor using contention protocol in case of failure.


Ring may have a single point of failure depending on the physical topology.

Use wiring concentrators to switch out faulty hosts.



Fibre-Distributed Data Interface
FDDI uses optical fibre cabling, which supports data transmission rates over long distances (about 100km).

Class A: attached both rings.

Any faulty class A will not affect the remaining class As.


Class B: attached to only one ring.

Any faulty class B affects one ring at maximum.



Medium Access Control
MAC is necessary to resolve frame collisions in broadcast channels, which comes with higher chance of frame collision.

In a wired network, frame collision result in both frames needing to be re-transmitted because the voltage is almost the same within a LAN.
In a wireless network, one signal may be stronger/weaker and a frame may be received even with collision.

Collisions are usually detected if broadcasting host does not receive an acknowledgement (ACK) for the frame after a certain amount of time.
Collisions are inevitable as there is no central authority controlling transmission. It is a best-effort service, in the worst case a frame may be indefinitely delayed.

Suitable for most LANs
Unacceptable for real-time systems.

Strategies for Medium Access Control

No control

Very inefficient.
Good if channel utilisation is low.


Round-robin

Stations take turns using the channel.
Used in token-based MAC systems


Reservations

Stations need to obtain channel reservation before transmitting.
Used in slotted MAC systems.


Token-passing

Token transferred from one station to another.
If a station has the token but no frame to send, pass token on immediately.
If a station has the token and a frame to send, it sets a timer and transmits until the timer expires or there is no more data to send.


Using switches

Each Ethernet cable acts as a channel, which has at most two stations (host and switch).
Switch ensures that collisions never occur by buffering frames and re-transmitting when the channel is available.
Switches act as repeaters, refreshing the signal to pass it further.


Static channel allocation

Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)

Not ideal if many stations exist.




Dynamic channel allocation

ALOHA protocol

Stations may transmit at any time.
When collision occurs, wait for a random time interval before retransmitting.
Fair channel access.
Low channel efficiency.
Large vulnerable period (i.e. new frames can easily destroy old frames).


Slotted ALOHA protocol

Stations may transmit only at the start of a specific time slot.
Small vulnerable period (i.e. collision only happens with exact overlap, assuming the time slot is long enough).
100% load gives 36% efficiency.


Carrier sensing

Stations may transmit only when channel is idle.
Collisions can still occur due to transmission delay.





Collision Detection for Medium Access Control
In collision detection, host listens to channel during transmission to check for collisions.

Transmission stops when collision detected, then sends a jamming signal to notify other stations of collisions.
Stations must transmit long enough to check if the frame collided.

Minimum frame length is  where  is a end-to-end transmission delay.



The minimum frame length for Ethernet is 512 bits. There a few ways to work around this limitation for small frames:

Career extension

Any frame less than 512 bits is extended by the host.


Frame bursting

Several short frames can be used to make one long frame.



Channel Back-off for Medium Access Control

1-persistent CSMA

Station keeps checking if the channel is free and then transmits immediately.


-persistent CSMA

Station keeps checking if the channel is free and then transmits at probability .


Non-persistent CSMA

If the channel is busy, wait for a random amount of time before checking again.


Binary exponential back-off

Minimum frame length is called a slot.
If a collision occurs in transmission, wait 0 or 1 slots before attempting again.
After  collisions, wait  to  slots, up to the limit of 1023 slots / 10 collisions.



Ethernet uses 1-persistent CSMA when network load is low, and binary exponential back-off when network load is high.
Address Resolution Protocol
A MAC address is enough for communication within a LAN, but an IP Address is required to communicate outside of the LAN.

IP addresses specify addresses of hosts involved in end-to-end communication
MAC addresses specify addresses of hosts involved in end-to-end communication within the same network.

Switches are in the data-link layer and cannot read the destination’s IP addresses in network layer, so they send an ARP request to routers to find out the MAC address.
IP Address can be set statically (fixed IP, set manually) or dynamically (assigned to your NIC, e.g by DHCP service).
IP Addresses specify hosts on the internet, it does not have to be translated when passing through a router (but can, e.g NAT).
MAC Addresses specify hosts communicating on the same network/subnetwork. Typically do not change when passing through routers (as packets).

Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/N.title: Network Layer
Computer-Science/Networks/To-Be-Sorted/N.content:
Network Layer
Internet
The Internet is a collection of autonomous systems connected together by backbones.
Internet Protocol
IP data are fragmented into datagrams, which are routed through the Internet:

Routers pass IP datagrams to the underlying data link layer (e.g. to LANs and WANs).
Switches pass Ethernet frames to the underlying physical layer.

Internet Protocol specifies:

Datagram format
Fragmentation
Addressing
Packet handling

Fragmentation in IP
Routers must handle cases where size of input datagram exceeds the MTU, which is identified by Path MTU Discovery. Datagrams may need to be fragmented further along the path.
The destination reassembles fragmented datagrams, not the intermediate routers.
IPv4 Address
IP addresses are managed by nonprofit ICANN to avoid conflicts.
ICANN delegates parts of address space to various regional authorities, which give out IP addresses to ISPs and companies.
Special IP addresses:

0.0.0.0/0

Represents any IP address.
Used in firewalls and servers to listen on any IP address.
Used in routing table for the default entry.


127.0.0.0/8

Loopback address.
Can send packets to this address.


169.254.0.0/16

Returned to indicate the absence of DHCP servers.



IP address can be assigned to a PC or router.
Each IP address is associated with an NIC, so it is possible for one host to have multiple IP addresses or share a single IP address with multiple hosts.
IPv4 Headers

Version (4 bits)
Internet Header Length (4 bits)

Size of IP header in 32-bit words.


Type of Service (8 bits)

Specifies how datagram should be handled by routers e.g. priority level.


Total Length (16 bits)

Total length of the packet in bytes (including the header).


Identification (16 bits)

Unique value assigned to each datagram used during fragmentation and reassembly.


Flags (3 bits)
Fragment Offset (13 bits)

Position of a fragment in the original un-fragmented packet.


Time to Live (8 bits)

Maximum number of hops a packet can take before it is discarded.


Protocol (8 bits)

Protocol used in the data portion of the packet.


Header Checksum (16 bits)

Checksum for header.


Source IP Address (32 bits)
Destination IP Address (32 bits)
Options field (variable)
Padding field (variable)

IPv6 Addresses
IPv6 keeps the main header as simple as possible and provides further information in optional extension headers.

Version (4 bits)
Traffic Class (8 bits)

Used to prioritise packets.


Flow Label (20 bits)

Used to identify packets that belong to the same flow.


Payload Length (16 bits)

Length of the packet’s payload (excluding the header).


Next Header (8 bits)

Protocol type of the next header after the IPv6 header.


Hop Limit (8 bits)

Similar to the Time to Live (TTL) field in IPv4.
Limits the number of hops a packet can take before being discarded.


Source Address (128 bits)
Destination Address (128 bits)

The following fields have been removed from IPv4:

Fragmentation

IPv6 pushes fragmentation onto end-systems for efficiency.


Header checksum

Redundant as IP header is small.
Available in extension headers.


Options

Fixed-length headers are easier to process.
Extension headers allows better modularity.



Routing Hierarchy
All interfaces in same subnet share the same address prefix so that routers outside the network can easily identify the subnet.

External routers only consider network address and forward the packet to one of the routers of the organisation.
Subnet routers apply the subnet mask, and look up if the destination is on their subnet, or if they should forward it to another subnet router
After host was found, router knows which interface to use to forward packet

Notations for addressing subnets:

Class notation

Class A: 8 bits
Class B: 16 bits
Class C: 24 bits


Prefix-length notation: address/prefix-length.

Known as Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR).


Mask notation: address/mask.

The smallest IP address in the subnet is used for identifying the subnet itself (e.g. for routing). The largest IP address in the subnet is the broadcast address for the subnet.
Routing

Routers must know the topology of the network to choose appropriate paths.
Routers must balance loads among routes.
Routers also has to deal with network heterogeneity.

Routers find the network address using subnet mask, and forward the packet based on the information found in the forwarding table.
The longest prefix match is used when the forwarding table contains multiple subnets that contain the destination IP address.
Routing Algorithms
Routers collaborate to build a sink tree of each node, which only contains the optimal routes towards that specific host.
Shortest Path Routing
Forward packets along the shortest path using Dijkstra’s algorithm.
Flood Routing
Forward packets across every outgoing link, except the original source.
Flood routing always chooses the shortest because all paths are explored in parallel, but the overhead is significant.
Some ways to minimise the overhead include:

Use a hop counter

Discard packet after forwarded  times.
Difficult to find the appropriate hop count.


Forward packets only once per router

Avoid building directed cycles.
Requires router to record packets it forwarded.


Flood selectivity

Forward packets only in some direction.



Distance Vector Routing
Distance vector routing is implemented by RIP protocol.
Forward packets along the shortest path using a variant of Bellman-Ford algorithm.
Distance vector routing is a dynamic protocol that takes network load into account.
Let  be the provisional distance from node  to .

Set cost to .
Update costs according to Bellman-Forward equation.
Advertise new costs to direct neighbours.
To forward a packet:

Check distance vector of direct neighbours.
Select the neighbour with smallest cost to destination.



Count-to-infinity problem arises when a node goes inactive (c.f. example).
Link State Routing
Link state routing is implemented by OSPF protocol.
Let  be a set of all weighted edges from node .

Calculate  for each .

Send HELLO packet to identify the neighbours of .


Advertise  to all other nodes.

Send ECHO packet to measure RTT from  to its neighbour.


To forward packet

Run Dijkstra’s algorithm locally.
Select neighbour on the shortest path.



A well-known issue is that routers may redirect traffic towards the best routes so much these routes become overloaded.
Broadcast Routing
Broadcast routing aims to send message to almost every host on the network
Let  denote the source host.

Broadcast packets to every neighbour.
Neighbour  only accept packets from  if shortest  path contains .

Neighbour  knows shortest  path from its routing table.



This algorithm is known as Reverse-path forwarding broadcast.
Multicast Routing
Multicast routing aims to send message to only a group of hosts on the network.

Select core for each group.
Source host sends message to the core whose group contains multicast targets.
Core sends message to its group.

Use broadcast routing with pruning.



Hierarchical Routing
Issues with flat network model:

Scalability

Hundreds of millions of hosts in today’s Internet
Transmitting routing information too expensive
Forwarding also too expensive


Administrative autonomy

Organisations may want to use different routing protocols within their network.
Organisations do not want to expose internal network structure



Hierarchical routing aims to find suboptimal routes by introducing regions, and separate algorithms for intra-region and inter-region routing.
Gateway routers interconnect autonomous systems.

Intra-AS routing protocol

Should provide optimal routing.


Inter-AS routing protocol

Only provides suboptimal routing.
e.g. Hot-potato routing, BGP



In hierarchical routing, both inter-AS and intra-AS routing information used to populate forwarding tables.
Open Shortest Path First
OSPF allows an AS to be divided into areas.

Use the same algorithm for the areas and the backbone.
One or more area border routers responsible for routing packets outside its area.
Backbone area routers redirect traffic between other areas in AS.

Border Gateway Protocol
BGP is path-vector protocol:

Based on distance vector routing.

But path vectors are advertised instead of distance vectors.


Does not suffer from count-to-infinity problem.

Router makes decision on the entire path.


Routers may aggregate prefixes (a.k.a. super-netting).

In BGP, neighbouring routers uses TCP connection to simplify reliability.

Provides reachability information from neighbour AS.
Transmits reachability information to all internal routers within AS.

Some BGP attributes:

ASN

Autonomous System Number.
Unique identifier for each AS.


AS-PATH

Sequence of ASNs through which advertisement was sent.
Advertised per destination AS.


NEXT-HOP

Specifies IP address for forwarding packets towards advertised destination.

Useful for when AS is reachable through multiple IP addresses.


Advertised per destination AS.



BGP import policy decides whether to accept or reject route advertisement
e.g. router may not want to send traffic through one AS listed in AS-PATH.
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol
Internet users receive an IP address from ISP via DHCP:

Each newly-booted machine broadcasts a DHCP DISCOVER packet.
DHCP server replies with the assigned IP address.
DHCP server can either maintain static mappings or assign different addresses each time a host connects.

Network Address Translation
NAT translates a single public IP address into many private IP addresses. It holds the mapping from the public port to the private IP address and its port.

Within a LAN, every computer gets a unique private IP address for routing internal traffic.
Address translation takes place when a packet exits the LAN.

The following address ranges are declared as private for use in local networks:

10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255/8 (16,777,216 addresses)
172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255/12 (1,048,576 addresses)
192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255/16 (65,536 addresses)

There are some criticisms of NAT:

NAT violates architectural IP model, which states that every IP address uniquely identifies single machine worldwide.
NAT changes Internet from connectionless to connection-oriented network.

NAT gateway must maintain mapping for each connection.


NAT violates fundamental rule of protocol layering.

NAT in the network layer is dependent on TCP in the transport layer.


Many P2P protocols require full connectivity among hosts.

Users behind NAT cannot be contacted directly by other peers.
Techniques exist to circumvent these issues (e.g. port forwarding).



IPv6 is the best solution to deal with the shortage of IP addresses.
Internet Control Message Protocol
ICMP is used to inform hosts and routers about errors and send queries about status information (e.g. ping).

Encapsulates control messages in IP packets .
Message type is specified by code (e.g. destination unreachable = 3).

Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.title: Device Management
Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.content:
Device Management
IO Management
Objectives of IO device management:

Fair access to shared devices.

Allocation of dedicated devices.


Exploit parallelism of I/O devices for multiprogramming.
Provide uniform simple view of I/O.

Hide complexity of device handling.
Give uniform naming and error handling



Device Classes
Devices are grouped into device classes:

Members of each device class perform similar functions.
Allows kernel to address performance needs of certain device class.

Major and minor identification numbers:

Used by device drivers to identify their devices.
Devices with same major number are controlled by same driver.
Minor numbers enable system to distinguish between devices of same class.

Device Independence

Device independence from

Device type (e.g. terminal, disk or DVD drive)
Device instance (e.g. which disk)
Device variations

Unit of data transfer (e.g. character or buffered block)
Supported operations (e.g. read, write, seek)
Synchronous or asynchronous operation
Speed differences
Sharable or single user (e.g. printer, DVD-RW)
Types of error conditions





Device Independent Layer
Device independent layer provides device independence:

Maps logical to physical devices (naming and switching).
Requests validation against device characteristics.
Allocation of dedicated devices.
User access validation.
Buffering for performance and block size independence.
Error reporting.

Device Driver
Device driver handles one device type, but may control multiple devices of same type:

Implements block read or write
Access device registers
Initiate operations
Schedule requests
Handle errors

Interrupt Handler

Device sends an interrupt to notify the process when operation is complete.
Interrupt handler processes these interrupts.

For block devices:

On transfer completion, signal device handler.


For character devices:

On character transfer, process next character.





Dedicated, Shared and Spooled Devices:

Dedicated device (e.g. DVD writer, terminal, printer)

Dedicated to a single process.
Open fails if already opened.

Alternatively, queue open requests.


Allocated for long periods.
Allocated only to authorised processes.


Shared device (e.g. disks, window terminals)

Shared by multiple processes.
OS provides file system for disks.


Spooled devices (e.g. printers)

Content is saved to disk file.
File printed later by spooler daemon.

Printer only allocated to spooler daemon.
No normal process allowed direct access.


Avoids blocking access to non-shareable devices.



Buffered, Unbuffered IO

Buffered IO:

Used to smooth peaks in IO traffic.
Caters for differences in data transfer units between devices.
For output:

User data transferred to OS output buffer.
Process continues and only suspends when buffer full.


For input:

OS reads ahead; reads normally satisfied from buffer.
Process blocks when buffer empty.




Unbuffered IO:

Data is transferred directly from user space to/from device.
Each read/write causes physical IO.

Implies device handler used for each transfer.
High process switching overhead.





User-Level IO Interface

IO operations

e.g. open, close, read, write, seek


OS library procedures to set up IO parameters

Must be device independent
Synchronous or asynchronous
Blocking or non-blocking



Unix allows user to access virtual devices as files.
Linux IO Management
Loadable Kernel Module
Linux’s LKM provides device drivers:

Contain object code, loaded on-demand

Dynamically linked to running kernel
Provided by hardware vendors or independent developers


Require binary compatibility

Modules written for different kernel versions may not work



Kmod is a kernel subsystem that manages modules without user intervention:

Determines module dependencies.
Loads modules on demand.

Device Special Files
Most devices in Linux are represented by device special files.

Entries in /dev directory provide access to devices.
List of devices in system can be obtained by reading contents of /proc/devices.

Virtual File System
Device files accessed via virtual file system:

System calls pass to VFS, which in turn issues calls to device drivers.
Most drivers implement common file operations.

e.g. read, write, seek



Linux IO Classes

Character (unstructured): Files and devices
Block (structured): Devices
Pipes (message): Interprocess communication
Socket (message): Network interface

Linux Character Device

Transmits data as stream of bytes.
Represented by char_device_struct structure which contains:

Driver name.
Registered major and minor numbers.
Pointer to driver’s file_operations structure.


All registered drivers referenced by chrdevs vector.

Linux Block Device
Kernel’s block IO subsystem contains a number of layers, which	modularises operations by placing common code in each layer.
There are two primary strategies used by kernel to minimise overhead of accessing block devices:

Caching data

Search cache when data from block device is requested.
If found, data is copied to process’s address space.
Otherwise, data is added to request queue.
Use direct IO to bypass kernel cache:

Important for databases and other applications
Kernel caching inappropriate and may reduce performance/consistency




Clustering IO operations

Linux Socket Device

Allows bidirectional communication.
Can be used to exchange information both locally and across a network.

Unlike pipes which are identified by machine specific file descriptors


Two types of sockets:

TCP (stream sockets)
UDP (datagram sockets)



File Descriptor Table
Each process has its own file descriptor table.


Each process has three file descriptors when created:

stdin (0)
stdout (1)
stderr (2)



By default, all three file descriptors refer to terminal from which program was started.


Blocking IO

IO call returns when operation completed
Process is suspended until the operation is completed.
Easy to understand but leads to multi-threaded code.

Prevent the program from being unresponsive.





Non-blocking IO

IO call returns as much as available (e.g. read with 0 bytes).
Turn on for file descriptor using fcntl system call.
Provides application-level polling for IO.

Application periodically checks to see if more data is available.





Asynchronous IO

Process executes in parallel with IO operation.

No blocking in interface procedure.


IO subsystems notifies process upon completion via callbacks or process signals.
Very flexible and efficient but harder to use and potentially less secure.

Timing and order of execution can be exploited.

Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.title: Disk Management
File Systems
Memory Management
Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.content:
Memory Management
Memory management binds logical address space to physical address space.


Logical address

Generated by the CPU.
Address space seen by process.



Physical address

Address seen by the memory unit.
Refers to physical system memory.



Compile-time and load-time address-binding scheme:

Logical address and physical address are the same.



Execution-time address-binding scheme:

Logical address and physical address are different.



Note that memory address is given in units of byte. Two hexadecimal digits represents a byte.
Memory Management Unit
MMU is a hardware device for mapping logical to physical addresses

User process deals with logical addresses only.
Implemented in hardware for speed.
e.g. Add value in relocation register to every address generated by process

Main memory usually split into two partitions:

Kernel space

Used by resident operating system.
Held in low memory with interrupt vector.


User space

Held in high memory.



Memory Allocation
Contiguous Memory Allocation
Contiguous allocation is done with relocation registers:

Base register contains value of smallest physical address for each process.
Limit register contains range of logical addresses for each process.
MMU maps logical address dynamically.

Multiple-Partition Allocation
A hole is a block of available memory. Holes of various size are often scattered throughout memory.
When a new process arrives, OS must allocate memory from hole large enough. For this purpose OS maintains information about:

Allocated partitions.
Free partitions (hole).

Dynamic Storage Allocation
Strategies for how to satisfy request of a certain size from list of free holes:

First-fit: Allocate first hole that is big enough
Best-fit: Allocate smallest hole that is big enough

Must search the entire list, unless ordered by size.
Produces smallest leftover hole.


Worst-fit: Allocate largest hole

Must search the entire list
Produces largest leftover hole.



Fragmentation

External fragmentation

There is enough free memory but not contiguous.


Internal fragmentation

Allocated memory larger than requested memory.
Due to size difference internal to partition.



External fragmentation can be reduced by compaction:

Rearrange memory to place all free memory together in one large block.
Leads to I/O bottlenecks.

Memory Protection
Check memory access is valid to protect user processes.

From each other.
From changing operating-system code and data.

Memory protection is implemented by associating protection bits with pages:

Valid-bit indicates legal page:

Associated page exists in logical address space.


Invalid-bit indicates page missing:

Associated page does not exit logical address space (page fault).
Need to load page from disk (c.f. demand paging).
Could be an incorrect access.



Virtual Memory
Ideas of virtual memory:

Separation of user logical memory from physical memory.

Only part of process needs to be in memory for execution.
Logical address space can be much larger than physical address space.
Address spaces can be shared by several processes.



Virtual memory can be implemented via:

Segmentation
Paging

Segmentation
Segmentation uses special registers for indicating the base address of memory for each process, which get updated on context switch:

Address space that can grow/shrink independently.
Support different kinds of protection (read/write/execute).
Unlike pages, programmers are aware of segments.

Segmentation provides separate address spaces for code, data, stack.

Code segment (CC)
Data segment (DS)
Extra segment (ES)
Stack segment (SS)

Paging
Logical address space of process can be noncontiguous in physical address. Process gets allocated physical memory when available.

Frames

Fixed-sized blocks of physical memory.
OS keeps track of all free frames.


Pages

Block of same size of logical memory.



To run program of size  pages:

Find  free frames and load program.
Set up page-table to translate logical to physical addresses.

Page-table is stored in kernel memory per process.
Page-table base register (PTBR) points to page table.
Page-table length register (PTLR) indicates size.
Each page contains address of its frame in physical memory.



User space address is divided into:

Page number ()

Used as index into page table.


Page offset ()

Combined with base address.



Kernel space address is simply mapped by offset, as this makes it easy to detect user access to kernel space.
Fixed-size paging avoids external memory fragmentation.
Page table stores an entry for every virtual page and can be extremely large. To address this issue we can use:

Hierarchical paging
Hashed page table
Inverted page table

Translation Look-aside Buffers
In paging, every data/instruction access requires two memory accesses.
TLB uses special fast-lookup hardware cache as associative memory:

If  in associative register, get frame address out.
Otherwise get frame address from page-table in memory.

TLB needs to be flushed after context switch, which can lead to substantial overhead.

Some TLBs store address-space ids in entries

Identifies each process to provide address-space protection for that process.
Allows kernel pages to be remain in TLB.



Effective Access Time for TLB
Assume memory cycle time is 1 microseconds and that:

Associative lookup takes  microseconds.
Hit ratio for associative memory is .

The effective access time (EAT) is given by:

Hierarchical Paging
Hierarchical paging breaks up logical address space into multiple page tables.

Two-level page table

First page table

Page number: 12 bits
Page offset: 20 bits


Second page table

Page number: 10 bits
Page offset: 10 bits





Hashed Page Table

Store entry based on hashed page numbers.

Hashed page number is the key.
Frame number is the value.


Search for match of hashed page number in key.
Use separate chaining for page numbers with the same hash.

Inverted Page Table

Store entry for all frame numbers.

Frame number is the key.
Page number is the value.


Search for match of page number in value.
Increases time to search table when page reference occurs.

Can use hash table to limit search.



Swapping

Swap processes temporarily out of memory to disk.
Bring back into memory for continued execution.
Swap space can be file or dedicated partition on disk.
Transfer time is the major part of swap time.

Use swapping when:

Number of processes is limited by amount of available memory
Only running processes need to be in memory.

Shared Memory
Processes can set up shared memory areas:

Implicitly or explicitly mapped to files on disk.
After shared memory is established, no need for kernel involvement.

Demand Paging
Demand paging brings page into memory only when needed:

Lower I/O load
Less memory needed
Faster response time
Support for more user

OS references a page on demand:

If entry not found, abort.
If valid bit set:

Page exists in memory.
Translate page number to frame number.


If valid bit not set:

Page does not exit in memory.
Page fault to bring page into memory.

Get empty frame.
Swap page into frame.
Set valid bit.
Restart last instruction.


Translate page number to frame number.



Effective Access Time for Demand Paging
Given page fault rate , the effective access time (EAT) is given by:

Copy on Write
Allows parent and child processes to initially share same pages in memory (e.g. fork()).

If either process modifies shared page, then copy page.
Efficient process creation as only modified pages are copied.
Free pages allocated from pool of zeroed-out pages.

fork() gives child its own page table pointing to parent’s read-only pages:

When any process writes to a page

Protection fault causes trap to the kernel.
Kernel allocates new copy of page so that both processes have their own private copies.
Both copies are marked read-write.



Memory Mapped Files

Map file into virtual address space using paging.
Simplifies programming model for I/O.

Page Replacement
Page replacement attempts to find some unused page in memory to swap out when there is no free frame available.
Strategies for efficient page replacement:

Minimise number of page faults.

Avoid bringing same page into memory several times.
Expects page faults to decrease with more frames.


Use dirty bit to reduce overhead of page transfers.

Only modified pages written to disk.



Reference string is a particular string of memory references, which is used to evaluate algorithms by computing number of page faults.
First-in-First-out Algorithm
FIFO algorithm replaces the oldest page in page table.

May replace heavily used page
Belady’s Anomaly

More frames can lead to more page faults.



Optimal Algorithm
Optimal algorithm replaces page that is unused for the longest period of time.

Impractical
Used for measuring the ideal performance.

Least-Recently-Used Algorithm
In LRU algorithm, each page entry has a counter:

When page is referenced, copy clock into counter.
When page needs to be replaced, choose the lowest counter.

Proper LRU is expensive, so approximations are used in practice:

Reference bit

Each page has reference bit , initially set .
When page is referenced, set .
When page needs to be replaced, choose page with  (if it exists).


Periodically reset reference bits.
Does not provide proper order for LRU.

Second Chance Algorithm
Second chance algorithm combines round-robin replacement with reference bits.

Traverse pages in round-robin order.
If page has :

Set .
Move to next page.


If page has :

Replace page.
Move to next page.



Least Frequently Used Algorithm
LFU algorithm replaces page with the smallest access count.

May replace page just brought into memory.
Never forgets heavy page usage.

Reset counters periodically.
Use aging.



Most Frequently Used Algorithm
MFU algorithm replaces page with the largest access count.

Replaces frequently used page.
Never forgets unused pages.

Working Set Clock Algorithm
Working set of pages  is a set of pages referenced by process during interval .

If page has :

Set .
Move to next page.


If page has :

Calculate the last time of use .
If  ( in working set):

Move to next page.


If  ( not in working set):

Replace page.
Move to next page.





The size of working set is determined by OS and may transition over time.
Global and Local Replacement

Local strategy

Each process gets fixed allocation of physical memory.
Need to pick up changes in working set size.


Global strategy

Dynamically share memory between runnable processes.
Initially allocate memory proportional to process size.
Consider page fault frequency to tune allocation.

Measure page faults per second.





There is no universally agreed solution as it depends on scheduling strategy.

CR0 (Control Register 0): This register controls the operation of the processor and contains various flags that control the processor’s behavior, including the flag that controls whether the processor is operating in user mode or kernel mode.
When a process makes a system call, the processor switches to kernel mode by changing the segment selectors in the CS, DS, and SS registers to point to kernel-level memory segments. The CR0 register is also updated to indicate that the processor is operating in kernel mode.

Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.title: Processes
Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.content:
Processes
Process allows a single processor to run multiple programs simultaneously.

Provide concurrency.
Provide isolation between programs.

Each process has its own address space.


Simplicity of programming.

No need to worry about behaviour of other programs.


Allow better utilisation of resources.

Different processes require different resources at certain times.



Types of Concurrency

Pseudo concurrency

Single processor is switched between processes by interleaving
Gives the illusion of concurrent execution over time.


Real concurrency

Utilises multiple physical processors.
Usually fewer processors than processes.



Context Switch
On a context switch, the processor switches from executing process to another process.

OS may switch processes in response to events or interrupts.
OS switches between processes cannot be pre-determined.

Because the events causing switches are non-deterministic.



It is important to avoid unnecessary context switches as they are expensive:

Direct cost

Save process state.
Restore process state.


Indirect cost

Perturbation of memory cache (TLB).

Typically flushed on a context switch.





Process Control Block
Information concerning the process is stored in a process descriptor or process control block (PCB):

Program counter
Stack pointer
Page table register
Process management information

Process ID
Parent process
Priority


File management information

Root directory
Current directory
Open file descriptors



Process Creation
Processes are created on:


System initialisation


User request


System call by a running process


Foreground process

Interacts with users.



Background process

Handles background jobs.
Known as daemons.



Process Termination

Normal completion

Process completes execution of body.


System call
* e.g. exit() in UNIX and ExitProcess() in Windows.
Abnormal exit

Process has run into an error.


Aborted

Process interrupted by another process.
e.g. killed from terminal


Never

Some processes run in endless loop
e.g. server applications



Process Hierarchies
UNIX allows processes to create process hierarchies:

When UNIX boots, it starts running init.
init forks off one process per terminal.
Forked processes wait for some user to login.
When login is successful, the login process runs a shell process to accept commands.
All processes in the system form a tree with init as root (i.e. process group).

Windows has no notion of hierarchy:

When a child process is created, the parent is given a handle for its control.
Handle can be passed to another processes.
Need to specify all parameters on creation.

Forking Process
Linux fork() creates new child process by making exact copy of parent process image.

Child process inherits resources of parent process.
Executed concurrently with parent process.
In parent process, returns PID of child.
In child process, returns 0.
On error, no child is created and returns -1.

Executing Process
Linux execve() runs the executable at the given path in a new process.

path specifies the full path of executable.
arg specifies the arguments passed to main().
envp specifies the environment variables.

Waiting for Process Termination
Linux waitpid() suspends execution of calling process until process with the given PID terminates.

PID of -1 tells the process to wait for any child.
PID of 0 tells the process to wait for any child in the same process group.
PID of -gid tells the process to wait for any child with the group id gid.
Returns PID of the terminated process.
Returns 0 if no children terminated.
Returns -1 on error.

Terminating Process
Linux exit() terminates a process.

Called implicitly when program finishes execution
Never returns in the calling process

Returns an exit status to the parent process



Linux kill() terminates a process and sends signal to process with the given PID.
Unix Signals
Unix signals are inter-process communication mechanism used to notify processes when event occurs.
Signals are generated:

When exception occurs.

e.g. division by zero (SIGFPE), segment violation (SIGSEGV)


When kernel wants to notify the process.

e.g. process writes to a closed pipe (SIGPIPE)


When certain key combinations are typed in a terminal.

e.g. keyboard interrupt (SIGINT)


Programatically by kill() system call.

Sending a signal requires a special permission (e.g. same user or super user). Kernel can send signals to any process.
Default action for most signals is to terminate the process, but  the receiving process may choose to:

Handle the signal by installing a signal handler with signal().
Ignore the signal.

SIGKILL and SIGSTOP cannot be ignored.



Unix Pipes
Unix pipe is a method of connecting the standard output of one process to the standard input of another.

Allows for one-way communication between processes.
Widely-used on the command line and in shell scripts.

Linux pipe() returns two file descriptors in fd:


fd[0]: read end of the pipe

Sender should close the unused read end.



fd[1]: write end of the pipe

Receiver should close the unused write end.



If receiver reads from empty pipe, it blocks until data is written at the other end.


If sender attempts to write to full pipe, it blocks until data is read at the other end.


Persistent pipes are stored on file system and can outlive process which created them. Any process can open persistent pipes like regular files.
Process has its own virtual machine

Own virtual CPU
Own address space (stack, heap, text, data etc.)
Open file descriptors

Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.title: Synchronisation
Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.content:
Synchronisation
Critical Section
Critical section is a section of code in which processes access a shared resource.

Synchronisation mechanism is required around the critical section.
Mutual exclusion

Ensures that a single process can execute code in the critical section.
Processes must request permission to enter the critical section.

No process may prevent other processes from entering the critical section.
No process requiring access to its critical section can be delayed forever.
No assumptions are made about relative the speed of processes.




Race condition

Multiple processes read and write shared data, leading to unexpected behaviour.



Synchronisation Mechanisms

Disabling interrupts

Thread interleaving is triggered by timer interrupts.

No interleaving occurs when interrupts are disabled.


Works only on single-processor systems.
Mechanism only used by kernel code.


Spin locks

Wastes CPU.
Should only be used when the wait is expected to be short.
May run into priority inversion problem.


Strict alternation

Processes take turns in executing the critical section.
Some processes may take a long time in non-critical sections.

Others must wait until these processes take turns.


c.f. Peterson’s solution


Semaphores
Locks
Monitors

Priority Inversion Problem
A lower-priority thread holds a resource needed by a higher-priority thread, causing the higher-priority thread to be blocked.
Lock Contention

Lock overhead

Measure of cost associated with using locks

Memory space
Initialisation
Time required to acquire and release locks




Lock contention

Measure of number of processes waiting for lock

More contention, less parallelism






























Coarse granularityFine granularityLock overheadLessMoreLock contentionMoreLessComplexityLessMore
Minimising Lock Contention

Release a lock as soon as not needed

Make critical sections small.


Use read/write locks

Process with the write lock has exclusive access.
Multiple processes can acquire the read lock.



Memory Models
Strong memory models assume sequential consistency:

Operations of each thread appear in program order.
Operations of all threads are executed atomically in some sequential order.

Producer/Consumer Pattern
www.tutorialspoint.com/producer-consumer-problem-using-semaphores

Producer constraints

Items can only be deposited in buffer if there is space
Items can only be deposited in buffer if mutual exclusion is ensured


Consumer constraints

Items can only be fetched from buffer if it is not empt
Items can only be fetched from buffer if mutual exclusion is ensured


Buffer constraints

Buffer can hold between 0 and N items.



Producer/consumer pattern can be implemented with semaphores or monitors.
Semaphores
Semaphores are special variables accessible with two private components:

A counter (non-negative integer).

Number of processes that can enter the critical section simultaneously.
c.f. Binary semaphore

Semaphore with counter initialised to 1.
Similar to locks.




A queue of processes currently waiting for the semaphore.

Semaphores are accessible via the following atomic operations:

down(s): receive a signal via semaphore s.
up(s): transmit a signal via semaphore s.
init(s,i): initialise semaphore s with i.

Monitors
Monitor is a high-level synchronisation primitive with the following components:

Shared data
Entry procedures
Internal procedures
Monitor lock

Used by entry/internal procedures


Condition variables

Condition variables are accessible via the following operations:

wait(c): releases monitor lock and waits for c to be signalled.
signal(c): wakes up one process waiting for c.

Signals do not accumulate in monitors.
If a condition variable is signalled with no one waiting for it, the signal is lost.


broadcast(c): wakes up all processes waiting for c.

Monitors are associated with high-level conditions, which are often language constructs.
Deadlocks
Coffman Conditions

Mutual exclusion

Each resource is either available or assigned to exactly one process.


Hold and wait

Process can request resources while it holds other resources earlier.


No preemption

Resources given to a process cannot be forcibly revoked.


Circular wait

Processes in a circular chain waiting for a resource held by each other.



Deadlock Detection

Directed graph can be used to model resource allocation.

Edge from resource to process means that the resource belongs to the process.
Edge from process to resource means that process is waiting for the resource to be released.


Cycle in the directed graph corresponds to a deadlock.

Deadlock Strategies

Ignore deadlocks

Only when contention for resources is low.


Dynamic avoidance

Only grant resource allocation when it is safe to do so.


Prevention

Ensure one of the four deadlock conditions can never hold.


Detection and recovery

Detect deadlock using DFS.
Recover from the deadlock by:

Pre-emption

Take the resource from owner temporarily and give it to another.


Rollback

Periodically check point the state of processes.
Roll back to the previous state on deadlock.
May repeat forever.


Killing process

Kill random process in the cycle.







Deadlock Prevention
Deadlock prevention by excluding one of the four deadlock conditions:

Exclude mutual exclusion condition:

Allow resources to be shared.


Exclude hold and wait condition:

Require all processes to request resources before starting.
Wait if not all resources are available.
Need to know what resources are required in advance.


Exclude no preemption condition:

Force process to give up resource.


Exclude circular wait condition:

Ensure process can hold at most one resource.
Force processes to take resource in predefined order.

Large number of resources can be difficult to organise.





Deadlock prevention in terms of lock acquisition:

Acquire locks atomically.

Acquire global lock before lock acquisition.


Give up lock on failure (e.g. pthread_mutex_trylock()).
Order lock acquisition.

Communication Deadlock

Process A sends message to B

A is blocked waiting for B


B did not get A message

A is blocked waiting for B.
B is blocked waiting for A.



Ordering resources nor careful scheduling is useful for communication deadlocks.
Communication protocol based on timeouts is necessary.
Livelock
Processes are not blocked but the system as a whole is not making progress:

pthread_mutex_trylock() failing forever.
Starvation of process with low priority.

Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.title: Threads
Computer-Science/Operating-Systems/To-Be.content:
Threads
Threads are execution streams that share the same address space. With multithreading each process can contain one or more threads.





























Per process itemsPer thread itemsAddress spaceProgram counterGlobal variablesRegistersOpen filesStackChild processesSignals
Motivations for using threads over processes:

Applications run multiple activities.

These activities execute in parallel and access the same data.


Processes are too heavyweight.

Blocking activity switches out the entire application.
Difficult to communicate between different address spaces.
Expensive to context switch between activities.
Expensive to create/destroy activities.



Concerns over use of threads:

Shared address space

Memory corruption

One thread can write another thread’s stack.


Concurrency bugs

Concurrent access to shared data (e.g. global variables).




Handling forks

Process can either:

Create a new process with the same number of threads.
Create a new process with a single thread.




Handling signals

Need to decide which thread should handle signals.



Creating Threads
Linux pthread_create() creates a new thread.

Created thread is stored in *thread.
Returns 0 on success, or error code.
attr specifies the thread attributes.
start_routine specifies the function to start execution.
arg specifies the argument passed to start_routine.

Terminating Threads
Linux pthread_exit() terminates the thread and makes *value_ptr available to any successful join with the terminating thread.
pthread_exit() is called implicitly when the thread’s start_routine returns.

Not for the initial thread which started main()
If main() terminates before other threads, the entire process is terminated.
If pthread_exit() is called in main() , the process continues executing until last thread terminates.

Yielding CPU
Linux pthread_yield() releases CPU to let another thread run.

Returns 0 on success, or an error code.
Always succeeds on Linux (not on Unix).

pthread_yield() is used for:

Busy waiting
Priority inversion

Give higher-priority thread chance to run by yielding after releasing a lock.
Particularly useful for non-preemptive scheduling.
Alternative to priority donation.



Joining Threads
Linux pthread_join() blocks until thread terminates.

thread is a handle given by pthread_create().
*value_ptr passed to pthread_exit() is available by *value_ptr.
value_ptr can be NULL.

User-Level Threads
OS kernel is not aware of user-level threads, and each process manages its own threads.


Threads implemented by software library.


Process maintains thread table for thread scheduling.


Better performance due to no kernel involvement.

Thread creation/termination are fast.
Thread switching is fast.
Thread synchronisation is fast (e.g. joining other threads).



Each application can have its own scheduling algorithm.


Blocking system calls block all threads in process.

Non-blocking IO are available but complicated.



Page faults block all threads in process.


Kernel-Level Threads
Kernel-level threads are managed by OS kernel.


Blocking system calls only block one thread.


Page faults block only block one thread.


Poor performance due to kernel involement.

Thread creation/termination more expensive.

Cheaper than process creation/termination.
OS may recycle threads using thread pools.


Thread synchronisation is more expensive.
Thread switching is more expensive.



No application-specific schedulers.

Computer-Science/Theory-of-Computation/I.title: Chapter 1

Engineering/Programming-Languages/Rust/R.title: Chapter 1 - Data Layout
Engineering/Programming-Languages/Rust/R.content:
Chapter 1 - Data Layout
Data Layout - The Rustonomicon (rust-lang.org)
Representation
repr(Rust) - The Rustonomicon (rust-lang.org)
Composite types:

Structs (named product types)
Tuples (anonymous product types)
Arrays (homogeneous product types)
Enums (sum types - tagged unions)
Unions (sum types - untagged unions)


A type’s size must always be a multiple of its alignment


Composite structures have an alignment equal to the maximum of their fields’ alignments.

Memory Alignment allows efficient memory/cache access.

Rust does guarantee that two instances of A have their data laid out in exactly the same way. However Rust does not currently guarantee that an instance of A has the same field ordering or padding as an instance of B.

This is unlike C where the ordering of members in a struct is the same as its definition.
In C this is not an issue because programmers can manually reorder its members to pack them as compactly as possible.
But this is a problem in Rust because of monomorphised and enum types - see examples in repr(Rust) - The Rustonomicon (rust-lang.org)

For monomorphised types it is kind of obvious.
For enum types this unspecified behaviour allows greater extent of optimisations like:

Representing None variant with a null pointer (without a tag).
Flattening the tags of nested enum types.



Exotically Sized Types
Exotically Sized Types - The Rustonomicon (rust-lang.org)
Dynamically Sized Types

Rust supports Dynamically Sized Types (DSTs): types without a statically known size or alignment. … Because they lack a statically known size, these types can only exist behind a pointer.

Common dynamically sized types (DSTs):

Trait objects e.g. dyn MyTrait
Slices e.g. [T] and str
Any structs with DST as their last field.

DSTs are sometimes known as wide pointers.

The information that completes a trait object pointer is the vtable pointer.

A trait object is composed of:

Pointer to data
Pointer to vtable, or method-lookup table (MLT)


The information that completes a slice pointer is just the number of elements it points to.

A slice is a view to array or Vec, and is composed of:

Pointer to array or Vec, which then points to data
The number of elements in the view


Structs can actually store a single DST directly as their last field, but this makes them a DST as well.

Storing a DST at the end of a struct is legal because the offset for each field will remain fixed in machine code, so there will be no performance loss.
Zero Sized Types

Rust also allows types to be specified that occupy no space.

Common zero-sized types (ZSTs):

Empty structs e.g. struct Nothing
Empty tuples e.g. ()
Empty array e.g. [u8; 0]
Any structs whose fields are all ZSTs

ZSTs are useful for pattern matching and more.
One of the examples presented in the doc is the implementation of Set with Map:

However in Rust, we can just say that Set<Key> = Map<Key, ()>. Now Rust statically knows that every load and store is useless, and no allocation has any size. The result is that the monomorphized code is basically a custom implementation of a HashSet with none of the overhead that HashMap would have to support values.

Pointers to ZSTs in unsafe code requires some care because they do not exist:

Safe code need not worry about ZSTs, but unsafe code must be careful about the consequence of types with no size. In particular, pointer offsets are no-ops, and allocators typically require a non-zero size.

Empty Types

Rust also enables types to be declared that cannot even be instantiated. These types can only be talked about at the type level, and never at the value level. Empty types can be declared by specifying an enum with no variants:

Empty types are akin to ZSTs in that they do not occupy space, but they can neither be instantiated.
enum Void {}
Use of empty types are primarily type-level:

For instance, suppose an API needs to return a Result in general, but a specific case actually is infallible. It’s actually possible to communicate this at the type level by returning a Result<T, Void>. Consumers of the API can confidently unwrap such a Result knowing that it’s statically impossible for this value to be an Err, as this would require providing a value of type Void.

In principle, Rust can exploit empty types to optimise code, not just for static analysis e.g. treating Result<T, Void> as T, but this is not implemented yet.
Other Representations
Attributes of the form repr(*) prepended to structs and enums tell Rust to use alternative data layout for primarily FFI and transmute

repr(C) - uses C ABI
repr(packed) - uses no padding
repr(align(n)) - uses n for alignment
