Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Any Type
Compute
Conditional Types
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Conditional Types
TypeScript: Documentation - Conditional Types (typescriptlang.org)


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
When conditional types act on a generic type, they become distributive when given a union type.

The distributive property only applies on the checked type T before extends, not the source type after extends:
type MapTypes1<T> = T extends unknown ? [T] : never
type MapTypes2<T> = never extends T ? [T] : never
 
type Type1 = MapTypes1<1 | 2> // [1] | [2], not [1 | 2]
type Type2 = MapTypes2<1 | 2> // [1 | 2], not [1] | [2]
The checked type T must be defined locally to be distributive:
type SomeKey = 1 | 2
type MapTypes1 = SomeKey extends unknown ? [SomeKey] : never
type MapTypes2 = 1 | 2 extends unknown ? [1 | 2] : never
type MapTypes3<T = 1 | 2> = T extends unknown ? [T] : never
type MapTypes4<T extends number[] = [1 | 2]> =
  T extends [infer Singleton]
    ? Singleton extends unknown ? [Singleton] : never
    : never
 
type Type1 = MapTypes1 // [1 | 2], not [1] | [2]
type Type2 = MapTypes2 // [1 | 2], not [1] | [2]
type Type3 = MapTypes3 // [1] | [2]
type Type4 = MapTypes4 // [1] | [2]
The expansion takes in-place at the extends clause:
type Wrap<T> = [T]
type MapTypes1<T> = Wrap<T extends unknown ? [T] : never>
type MapTypes2<T> = [T] extends [never]
  ? true
  : T extends unknown ? [T] : never
 
type Type1 = MapTypes1<1 | 2> // [[1] | [2]], not [[1]] | [[2]]
type Type2 = MapTypes2<1 | 2 | never> // [1] | [2], not [1] | [2] | true i.e. not MapTypes2<1> | MapTypes2<2> | MapTypes<never>
And last not but least, it seems like the Mapped Types also have the distributive property, although not explicitly documented:


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
This might be a bug or an intentional consequence of the original design, but mapped types do not distribute over union types.
Mapped Types should distribute over union types · Issue #28339 · microsoft/TypeScript (github.com)

type Compute<T> = { [P in keyof T]: T[P] }
type Type1 = Compute<{ key: string } | { key: number }> // Compute<{ date: string }> | Compute<{ date: null }>
Applications of Distributive Property
Distributive property of conditional types have several applications which can be a bit subtle:

Exclude and Extract:

type Exclude<T, U> = T extends U ? never : T;
type Extract<T, U> = T extends U ? T : never;
 
type Type1 = Exclude<"a" | "b" | "c", "a"> // "b" | "c"
type Type2 = Extract<"a" | "b" | "c", "a" | "d"> // "a"

Looping over union:

type Loop<T> = T extends unknown ? `Loop: ${T}` : never
 
type Type1 = Loop<1 | 2 | 3> // "Loop: 1" | "Loop: 2" | "Loop: 3"

Looping over union + collecting results by merging:

type IsUnion<T, U = T> =
  T extends unknown
    ? [U] extends [T] ? false : true
    : never
 
type Type1 = IsUnion<1 | 2> // false | false => false
type IsLiteralOf<T, U> = (U extends T ? false : true) extends true ? true : false
 
type Type1 = IsLiteralOf<"a", string | number>; // true
type Type2 = IsLiteralOf<string, string | number>; // false
Avoiding Distributive Property
We can avoid this distributive property by not making the comparison with the checked type on its own.


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
Typically, distributivity is the desired behavior. To avoid that behavior, you can surround each side of the extends keyword with square brackets.

The documentation make it seem like a syntactic construct, but it is “the comparison of T on its own” that really matters:
type IsNever1<T> = [T] extends [never] ? true : false // the checked type is [T], not T
type IsNever2<T> = T[] extends never[] ? true : false // the checked type is T[], not T
type IsNever3<T> = T extends never ? true : false // does not work
 
type Type1 = IsNever1<never> // true 
type Type2 = IsNever2<never> // true
type Type3 = IsNever3<never> // never
This is particularly useful when T can be never like this case, because IsNever3<T> never gets expanded due to the distributive property.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Empty Objects
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Empty Objects
Checking if T is an empty object type:
type IsEmptyObject1<T> = T extends {} ? true : false // always `true`
type IsEmptyObject2<T> = T extends Record<PropertyKey, never> ? true : false // works correctly
IsEmptyObject1<T> does not work because type {} contains objects with extra properties.
IsEmptyObject2<T> works because an object property can never have type never if defined, so Record mapping to type never effectively only allows the empty object {}.


                  
                  Note
                  
                
The global type PropertyKey refers to string | number | symbol.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Index Signatures
Indexing
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Indexing
TypeScript: Documentation - Indexed Access Types (typescriptlang.org)
The following types can be indexed by types:

Object types
Record types
Tuple types
Array types

type ObjectType = { key1: string; key2: number };
type RecordType = { [key: string]: number };
type TupleType = [string, number];
type ArrayType = string[];
 
type Type1 = ObjectType["key1"] // string
type Type2 = RecordType[string] // number
type Type3 = TupleType[0] // string
type Type4 = ArrayType[number] // string
We can see Mapped Types to be the dual of indexing.

Indexing by a union type is distributive:
type Person = { age: number; name: string; alive: boolean };
 
type Type1 = Person["age"]; // number
type Type2 = Person["age" | "name"]; // Person["age"] | Person["name] => number | string
Indexing a union type is also distributive:
type StringDate = { date: string };
type NumberDate = { date: number };
 
type Type1 = (StringDate | NumberDate)["date"]; // string | number

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Inference
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Inference
TODO
TypeScript: Documentation - Conditional Types (typescriptlang.org)
Inference is essentially pattern matching available in conditional types (on the RHS of extends)
It is worth noting that, in a union type, the same inferred type variable can appear more than once on the RHS of the same extends in a conditional type:
type Whitespace = ' ' | '\n' | '\t'
type Trim<S extends string> = S extends `${Whitespace}${infer Rest}` | `${infer Rest}${Whitespace}` ? Trim<Rest> : S
In such cases the first matching pattern in a union will be inferred.

Also, inference with consecutive type variables inside a template literal type works by simply binding the first character to the first type variable:
type Capitalize<S extends string> = S extends `${infer Char}${infer Rest}` ? `${Uppercase<Char>}${Rest}` : S;
The behaviour changes when there is a delimiter etc in between the two type variables, but the rule of thumb is to bind the first-matching parts of string type.
Furthermore, it is possible to use string inside template literal types:
type StringToTuple<S extends string> = S extends `${string}${infer Rest}` ? [0, ...StringToTuple<Rest>] : []

Spread operator doesn’t have the same restriction at the type level:
const [...init, last] = [1, 2, 3] // error
type Last<T extends any[]> = T extends [...unknown[], infer Last] ? Last : never // ok - spread operator not at the end of array

Beware some operations on unknown results in a tricky behaviour:
type Type1 = [string, ...never] // never, not `[string]`
type Type2 = `abc${never}` // never, not `"abc"`

It is also possible to add extends constraint for each inference variable:
type PercentageParser<A extends string> =
	A extends `${infer Sign extends '+' | '-'}${infer Rest}` ? [Sign, ...PercentageParser<Rest>] : ...

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Intersection and Union
Keyof
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Keyof
TypeScript: Documentation - TypeScript 2.1 (typescriptlang.org)


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
keyof T yields the type of permitted property names for T

But this “permitted” property names can be quite subtle for non-object types:
type ObjectKeys = keyof { key1: "abc", key2: "def" }; // "key1" | "key2"
type RecordKeys = keyof { [key: number]: Person }; // number
type ArrayKeys = keyof (string | number)[]; // "length" | "push" | "pop" | "concat" | ...
type TupleKeys = keyof [string, number]; // "length" | "push" | "pop" | "concat" | ...
 
type FuncKeys = keyof (() => any) // never
type NumberKeys = keyof number // "toString" | "toFixed" | "toExponential" | ...
type StringKeys = keyof string // "charAt" | "charCodeAt" | "concat" | ...
type AnyKeys = keyof any // string | number | symbol
type ObjectKeys = keyof object // never

In JavaScript, numeric strings (e.g. "0" and "99") and their corresponding numbers are interchangeable in object keys:
const obj1: { 0: any } = { "0": true } // ok
const obj2: { "0": any } = { 0: true } // ok
This is because we can omit quotes around string in object keys, which makes it impossible to tell if 0 as key is meant to be a number or string.
This nuance is reflected in the keyof record types:
type RecordKeys1 = keyof { [key: number]: Person }; // number
type RecordKeys2 = keyof { [key: string]: Person }; // string | number
But not in the keyof object types:
type ObjectKeys1 = keyof { 0: any }; // 0, not 0 | "0"
type ObjectKeys2 = keyof { "0": any }; // "0", not "0" | 0

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Literal Types
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Literal Types
TypeScript: Handbook - Literal Types (typescriptlang.org)
Strings, numeric (including floating numbers) and boolean literals can be a type on its own.
Template Literal Types
TypeScript: Documentation - Template Literal Types (typescriptlang.org)
Putting union types in template literal types results in a cross product:
type A = "a" | "b"
type B = "c" | "d"
 
type C = `${A}${B}` // "ac" | "ad" | "bc" | "bd"
Numeric Operations
Typescript Numeric Literal Types How To X + Y (Summation) - DEV Community
Most numeric operations are not supported in TypeScript, but there are some ways to simulate such results using tuples and its length property.
For instance, numeric types X and Y can be computed by:

Create tuples of length X and Y respectively, and;
Combine the two arrays and get the length of it

This technique works because it is a tuple type - string or array have the same length property but these will simply give number, not 1 or 2.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Local Variables
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Local Variables
Although we cannot define a local variable inside a type definition, we can achieve a similar effect by using the default parameter:
type UnionToTuple<T, L = LastOf<T>> =
  [T] extends [never] ? [] : [...UnionToTuple<Exclude<T, L>>, L];
In this example, L is not passed by the caller at all - it is only used to define a local variable L which is used in multiple occurrences.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Mapped Types
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Mapped Types
Readonly Modifier
readonly is a modifier for array and tuple types, and the properties of objects and records.


                  
                  Note
                  
                
const assertions make arrays readonly tuples, and the properties of objects readonly

readonly T is a superset of T for array and tuple types:
let a: readonly any[] = []
let b: any[] = []
 
a = b // ok
b = a // error
But this is not true for the properties of objects and records:
let a: { readonly key: string } = { key: "" }
let b: { key: string } = { key: "" }
 
a = b // ok
b = a // ok
Optional Modifier
Object types with optional properties { key?: T } is a superset of { key: T }.
let a: { key?: string } = { }
let b: { key: string } = { key: "" }
 
a = b // ok
b = a // error
It is tempting to see type { key?: T } as the set consisting of the empty object {} plus all objects in type { key: T }, but this contradicts the following example:
let a: { key1: string } & { key2?: string } = { key1: "" }
let b: { key1: string } = { key1: "" }
 
a = b // ok
The intersection of the types { key1: string } and { key2?: string } would not contain { key1: string } alone if this was the case - which is not what we want.
Instead, a more precise notion would be to think of readonly/optional modifiers to be a syntactic construct.
Keyword Remapping via as
TypeScript: Documentation - Mapped Types (typescriptlang.org)
type MappedTypeWithNewProperties<Type> = {
    [Properties in keyof Type as NewKeyType]: Type[Properties]
}
as keyword in mapped types are useful because it allows us to:

Create new property names from prior ones.
Filter out keys by producing never via a conditional type.
Map over arbitrary unions, not just unions of string | number | symbol.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Tail Recursion
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Tail Recursion
Tail recursive type constructors are not only efficient but seem to have a large recursion limit during instantiation, according to this example:
type UnionUpTo1<T extends number, Count extends any[] = []> =
  Count['length'] extends T ? never : Count['length'] | UnionUpTo1<T, [0, ...Count]>
type UnionUpTo2<T extends number, Count extends any[] = [], Acc = never> =
  Count['length'] extends T ? Acc : UnionUpTo2<T, [0, ...Count], Count['length'] | Acc>
 
type Type1 = UnionUpTo1<10> // ok
type Type2 = UnionUpTo1<100> // error - Type instantiation is excessively deep and possibly infinite.
type Type3 = UnionUpTo2<100> // ok
type Type4 = UnionUpTo2<1000> // error - Type instantiation is excessively deep and possibly infinite.


                  
                  Tip
                  
                
Tail recursive functions are more efficient because:

No need to keep execution stack once the next function is called, or even;
Can be simplified into a while loop.


Apparently 1000 is the recursion limit for type constructors in TypeScript, although some people have managed to bypass this limit:
TypeScriptの型で遊ぶ時、再帰制限を無効化する | Kgtkr’s Blog

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Type Equality
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Type Equality
TypeScript does not have an equality operator, so there are some well-known implementation for the utility type Equals<X, Y>.
A simple, no-brainer implementation would be:
export type Equals<X, Y> = 
	X extends Y
		? (Y extends X ? true : false) 
		: false
However this fails for any, because for every type T, any is both the superset and subset of T - so any extends T and T extends any.

A more complete implementation is:
github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/issues/27024#issuecomment-421529650
export type Equals<X, Y> =
    (<T>() => T extends X ? 1 : 2) extends
    (<T>() => T extends Y ? 1 : 2) ? true : false;
Since the conditional type T extends X ? 1 : 2 depends on type variable T, which is not resolved, this gets deferred:


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
A conditional type T extends U ? X : Y is either resolved to X or Y, or deferred because the condition depends on one or more type variables.
TypeScript: Documentation - TypeScript 2.8 (typescriptlang.org)

This implementation relies on the TS compiler’s internal functionality to check type equality, which is required to test relationship of two deferred conditional types - introduced in [this PR](conditional types type A is not assignable to type B · Issue #22860 · microsoft/TypeScript (github.com)).
In a nutshell, for arbitrary T, (T extends X ? 1 : 2) extends (T extends Y ? 1 : 2)  iff types X and Y are identical, because:

Assume by contradiction, that X and Y are not identical.
Without the loss of generality, Y does not contain X, so there exists a type U which is a subset of X but not Y.
Since T is arbitrary, if T may be U, but then U extends X ? 1 : 2 is 1 and U extends Y ? 1 : 2 is 2 - contradiction.


Some extra comments on variance:
github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/27118#issuecomment-424167550

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Type Hierarchy
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Type Hierarchy
Types are just data — Type-Level TypeScript (type-level-typescript.com)


                  
                  Tip
                  
                
A type is simply a set of values.
(This is consistent with the definition in typed first-order logic.)


For example:

Literal types such as 1 or "abc" contain values 1 and "abc", respectively.
Primitive types such as number or string contain primitive number/string values e.g. 3.14, "abcde", respectively.
Array types such as number[] contain array of numbers e.g. [1,2,3], [2.17]
Tuple types such as  [number, string] contain 2-tuples of numbers and string e.g. [1, "abc"], [-1, "p"]
Object types such as { key1: string } contain objects with property key1 of type string, plus any extra properties i.e. { key1: string, key2: number } is assignable to { key1: string }
Record types such as { [key: string]: number } contain objects with properties of the same key/value types e.g. { key1: number, key2: number }



                  
                  Info
                  
                

unknown is the type representing the set of all values

i.e. Any type T is assignable to unknown
i.e. Conversely, unknown is not assignable to any type.


never is the type representing the empty set i.e. no type T is assignable to never

e.g. Return type of a function that never terminates





void is the conventional type used to represent the return type of a function without return value

Only void and undefined are assignable to void
For any type T, () => T is assignable to () => void

Similarly for functions with parameters




any is the conventional type used to circumvent type checks (not uknown)

any can be seen as the superset and subset of every type T

Except for never, where any is not subset of never

never really is the empty set!


This makes any type T assignable to any and vice versa








                  
                  Warning
                  
                
Although type { key1: string } contains objects with extra properties other than property key1 of type string, TypeScript doesn’t allow these extra properties during initialisation to avoid possible errors e.g. when assigning object literals

This can be verified by the following example:
const a: { key1: string } = { key1: "", key2: 0 } // error
 
let b: { key1: string }
b = { key1: "", key2: 0 } // error
 
let c = { key1: "", key2: 0 } 
b = c // ok - not initialisation

Array types are superset of homogeneous tuple types:
let a: string[] = ["a"];
let b: [string] = ["a"]
 
a = b // ok
b = a // error - a may have less than or more than 1 element of type `string`
Similarly, record types are superset of homogeneous object types:
let a: { [k: string]: string } = { key: "a" }
let b: { key: string } = { key: "a" }
 
a = b // ok
b = a // error - a may not have property named `key`
Note that tuples/objects can be made homogeneous by simply taking the union of all its element/property types, so this is trivial.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Union to Tuples
Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.content:
Union to Tuples
Converting tuple types to union types is trivial:
type TupleToUnion<T extends any[]> = T[number]
But the converse is not true:
TypeScript で union to tuple をするのが難しい理由 (zenn.dev)
The method explained in the post makes use of the behaviour of Overload Signatures, where the last signature of the intersection is picked for making inferences.
This method consists of the following steps:

Convert A | B | C to () => A | () => B | () => C.
Convert () => A | () => B | () => C to () => A & () => B & () => C.
Pick the last signature using the above method, and add it to accumulator tuple.
Repeat 1-3 until the intersection becomes empty.

// (1)
type UnionToIntersection<U> =
  (U extends unknown ? (x: U) => void : never) extends (x: infer I) => void ? I : never;
 
// (2)
type LastOf<U> =
  UnionToIntersection<U extends unknown ? () => U : never> extends () => infer R ? R : never;
 
// (3)
type UnionToTuple<T, L = LastOf<T>> =
  [T] extends [never] ? [] : [...UnionToTuple<Exclude<T, L>>, L];
 
type Type = UnionToTuple<"a" | "b" | "c">; // ["a", "b", "c"]
(1) - In this example, by distributive property U extends unknown ? (args: U) => void : never expands to (((x: () => "a") => void) | ((x: () => "b") => void)) | ((x: () => "c") => void))


                  
                  Info
                  
                
In TypeScript, the parameters of these signatures are intersected together to create a new signature (x: (() => "a") & (() => "b") & (() => "c"))) => void.
TypeScript: Documentation - TypeScript 3.3 (typescriptlang.org)

(2) - This LastOf makes use of the U extends unknown ... technique to loop over union types and pass each item to UnionToIntersection. In this example, by distributive property U extends unknown ? () => U : never expands to (() => "a") | (() => "b") | (() => "c")).
(3) - This UnionToTuple makes use of the [T] extends [never] ... technique to prevent the distributive property of conditional types.

Engineering/Programming-Languages/TypeSc.title: Void Type

Engineering/Version-Control/Editing-Past.title: Editing Past Commits
Engineering/Version-Control/Editing-Past.content:
Editing Past Commits
Imagine we have a commit graph like this:
A---B---C (feature, origin/feature)

and we want to edit the commit B.
Rebasing a Temporary Branch
amending a single file in a past commit in git - Stack Overflow

Create a temporary branch e.g. temp from the commit B.

A---B (temp)
	 \
	  C (feature, origin/feature)


Edit the files.
Add the files to the index and amend the commit B.

A---B' (temp)
 \
  B---C (feature, origin/feature)


Rebase the feature branch onto the temp branch.

A---B' (temp)
 \   \
  B   C (feature, origin/feature)


Delete the temp branch.

Using git commit --fixup
git commit —fixup が便利Git - Qiita
fixup is a command you can use in interactive rebase.
It is like squash in that it melds into the previous commit, but keeps only the previous commit’s commit message.
Given a commit history like this, assume we want to edit commit Add feature 1:
9c57cb3e7a Add feature 2
ab6a6bef9d Add feature 1
cgbaeef9d3 Initial commit


Run git commit --fixup ab6a6bef9d to create a new !fixup commit:

26a2dc16a8 !fixup Add feature 1
9c57cb3e7a Add feature 2
ab6a6bef9d Add feature 1
cgbaeef9d3 Initial commit


Run git rebase -i --autosquash <commit>~1 to squash Add feature 1 and the corresponding !fixup commits:

1131338365 Add feature 2
9c5a2d3e7a Add feature 1
cgbaeef9d3 Initial commit



                  
                  Tip
                  
                
--autosquash squashes commits whose commit message starts with !fixup, and melds them into the commit with the same commit message.

This is also useful during PRs - reviewers can check the !fixup commits, and squash them when merging:
git commit —fixupを使いましょう - Don’t Repeat Yourself (blog-dry.com)

Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Hooks.title: Git Hooks
Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Hooks.content:
Git Hooks
Git - Git Hooks (git-scm.com)
Git hooks are scripts that are executed on specific events e.g. pre-commit, pre-push.
By default, hooks are stored under .git/hooks, and Git executes the script with the file name corresponding to the event e.g. for pre-commit events, .git/hooks/pre-commit.
If you need to run multiple scripts on a single event, you need to source them from .git/hooks/pre-commit.
Sharing Hooks
チームで共通のフックスクリプト(Git hook)を使っています - ファーエンドテクノロジー株式会社 (farend.co.jp)
By default, git hooks are local and not committed to the version control.
While this makes sense for some events, oftentimes we want to share hooks with the dev team.


                  
                  Example
                  
                
For instance, it is a good idea to let people to customise pre-commit hooks because this allows them to:

Commit partially working code for now and;
Squash or rebase commits later before pushing to remote

But we should probably use the same pre-push hooks to check formatting etc.

A common workaround is to store hooks under a separate directory e.g. .githooks, and configure git to use it instead of .git/hooks by running:
git config --local core.hooksPath .githooks

Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Internal.title: Git Internals
Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Internal.content:
Git Internals
Git - Git Objects (git-scm.com)
There are three types of git objects:

Blob objects
Tree objects
Commit objects

Unlike csv or subversion which keeps track of changes, git manages snapshots of tree/blob objects. This makes it significantly faster to switch or merge branches.
Git calculates the 40-character checksum hash (SHA1) of these objects based on their content
e.g. d670460b4b4aece5915caf5c68d12f560a9fe3e4.


                  
                  Tip
                  
                
You can often just specify the first 5-6 characters of the hash in the CLI, as long as it is unambiguous.

These objects are named after their hash and stored under .git/objects as zlib file.
For organisational purposes, they are grouped by subdirectory corresponding to the first two characters of their hash e.g.  .git/objects/d6/70460b4b4aece5915caf5c68d12f560a9fe3e4.


                  
                  Warning
                  
                
Git objects with the same content e.g. blob objects with the same content will have the same hash and will be stored in the same file under .git/objects.

Blob Objects
A blob object represents a single file in the working tree.
The content of a blob object is simply the file content e.g.
def main():
	print("Hello, world!")
Note that because the hash of a git object is based on their content only, two files with different file names but with the same content will get the same hash and thus correspond to the same blob object.
Tree Objects
A tree object represents a single directory in the working tree.
The content of a tree object looks like:
100644 blob a906cb2a4a904a152e80877d4088654daad0c859      README
100644 blob 8f94139338f9404f26296befa88755fc2598c289      Rakefile
040000 tree 99f1a6d12cb4b6f19c8655fca46c3ecf317074e0      lib



                  
                  Tip
                  
                
This is similar to how inode-based file systems store files.


The first column represent the mode of each file/directory.

This is similar to the output of ls -l.
The first digit represents directory (0) or file (1)
The second digit represents symlink (1) or not (0)
The remaining digit represents permission of each group i.e. 4 for write, 2 for read and 1 for execute.


The second column is the hash of each file/directory.
The last column is the name of each file/directory.

Note that modifying a file and committing it will necessarily update all of its parent tree objects.
Also, renaming a file/directory changes the corresponding tree object, not the blob object.
Commit Objects
A commit object represents a single commit.
The content of a commit object looks like:
tree d8329fc1cc938780ffdd9f94e0d364e0ea74f579
parent ca70291031230dde40264d62b6e8d2424e2c9366
author Scott Chacon <schacon@gmail.com> 1243040974 -0700
committer Scott Chacon <schacon@gmail.com> 1243040974 -0700

This is a commit message.

This is straightforward. A commit object points to the parent commit object, the tree object corresponding to the working tree, plus meta info and commit message.
A commit history is the graph formed by these commit objects and the references to their parents.


                  
                  Info
                  
                
To visualise, you can think of a DAG of trees of tree/blob objects, where these trees are overlapping with each. Git is a snapshot-based version control system, but this overlap makes it significantly space-efficient.

Note that because the commit message is part of a commit object, editing a commit message will change the commit object hash and thus any child commits, even if the working tree of the commit has not changed at all.
Branches and Tags
Branches and tags are simply pointers to a commit object.
Internally, branches are stored as text files (not zlib because the size is small) under .git/refs/heads, and tags under .git/refs/tags, with the file name corresponding to their branch/tag name.
The content of such file is simply the hash of the commit object they point to:
c6af3bdd357f57a9205d0ec75b350fdd553d5a940

The difference between branches and tags is that tags are static once created, while branches are updated to the latest commit when a new commit is created after HEAD.


                  
                  Info
                  
                
These tags directly pointing to a commit object are called lightweight tags.
Alternatively you can create an annotated tag which will point to a tag object instead. This tag object will contain the reference to the commit object and other meta information i.e. annotations.

Remote tracking branches are named like <remote>/<branch>, and are stored under .git/refs/remotes e.g. .git/refs/remotes/origin/main for origin/main. Similarly for tags.
HEAD file
The HEAD file is a text file which stores information about which commit is currently checked out.
The content of the HEAD file looks like:
ref: refs/heads/main

If the main branch is checked out. If the head is detached (i.e. not on any branch), it may look like this instead:
9388f3aca7f5f003cfd0e1b7b5f7dffd2331d432

index File
The index file represents the index or staging area of a git repository.
The index file keeps the list of tree/blob objects that have changed from the working tree of HEAD, and its content looks pretty much like a tree object:
100644 c6af3bdd357f57a9205d0ec75b350fdd553d5a940	contests/abc341/d/test/sample-3.out
100644 987ee7a91eb97f45b5354952279b71c7699a8d180	requirements.txt
100644 9b2eb6974ba74fead19b506d9cba34e1f3d7d3be0	temp.json

The only different is that the last column stores the file path instead of file name.
config File
The config file is an .ini file that simply stores the local config of a git repository, which looks like:
[core]
	repositoryformatversion = 0
	filemode = true
	bare = false
	logallrefupdates = true
	ignorecase = true
	precomposeunicode = true
[branch "main"]
	remote = origin
	merge = refs/heads/main
[remote "origin"]
	url = git@github.com:taichimaeda/markpilot.git
	fetch = +refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*
The config file also stores information like the remote repository and upstream branch you would set with git push --set-upstream.
Note that this config is per repository - the global config can be found at ~/.gitconfig.
logs Directory
The .git/logs directory contains the history of the commit objects pointed by pointers like HEAD, main and origin/main.
In addition to the commit hashes, these log files also keep a record of the action e.g. checkout: moving from main to feature/1.
Commands
git add
For each given file/directory, this command creates a new tree/blob object if the file/directory has changed from the working tree in the HEAD, and records this changed object in the index file.
It may reuse objects if already found under .git/objects.
git commit
This command checks the index file updated by git add, and creates a new commit object based on this index file, with the HEAD as its parent.
It recursively traverses the working tree of the HEAD from the root tree object, and replaces the current tree object with a new one if it contains a file/directory from the index file.
git commit --amend
This is the same as git commit in that it compares the HEAD with the index file to create a new tree representing the working tree, but the new commit object created with git commit --amend has HEAD~1 as its parent, not HEAD.
Note that this does not modify the existing commit object pointed by HEAD, but rather creates a new commit diverging from the parent of the HEAD.
git fetch
This command simply fetches the git objects from the corresponding upstream branch in the remote repository, and updates the remote tracking branches.
git pull
This is equivalent to git fetch <remote>/<branch> and  git rebase <remote>/<branch> or git merge <remote>/<branch>, depending on the config.
You can change this config by e.g. git config pull.rebase true.
git checkout
This changes the HEAD to the branch or a specific commit provided in the argument, and updates the working tree to that of the checked out commit.
Confusingly, this command is overloaded with many other uses, but these days most of them have an equivalent, domain-specific command like:

git restore for restoring a file
git switch for switching the branch

git reset
This moves the current branch so that it points to the given commit, and also clears the index by the default --mixed option.
If you git reset to a parent commit, this effectively resets all the changes made after that commit, but you can also git reset to a commit that is not a parent of the HEAD.
git reset has three options which have very, very semantic naming:

git reset --hard

Moves the branch, clears the index and updates the working tree.


git reset --mixed (default)

Moves the branch and clears the index, but keeps the working tree.


git reset --soft

Only moves the branch, but keeps the index and the working tree.
The index is updated so that it represents the changes from the given commit, instead of the HEAD.



This is a bit similar to git checkout in that they both update the working tree, but the difference is that git checkout changes the HEAD whereas git reset changes the branch the HEAD points to.
git merge
git merge <branch> merges <branch> into the current branch, where <branch> is typically a feature branch.
It creates a new merge commit H with the two parents C and G, and updates the current branch:
  A---B---C (<branch>)
 /
D---E---F---G (HEAD - main)

  A---B---C (<branch>)
 /         \
D---E---F---G---H (HEAD - main)

Unless the G can be fast-forward to C:
			  A---B---C (<branch>)
			 /
D---E---F---G (HEAD - main)

By default, git merge uses three-way merge algorithm called ort, which roughly works like:

git merges changes, not files


First, find the common base of target and source branches i.e. the HEAD and <branch>.

Or put more precisely, pick the LCA of target and source commits.


Identify the changes between the base and target.
Similarly, identify the changes between the base and source.
Finally, merge these changes with:

Chunks that have not changed in neither target or source.
Chunks that have changed in either target or source, but not both.
Chunks that have changed in both target and source in the same way.
Placeholders for chunks that have changed in both target and source in different ways, to be resolved by the user manually.

This can be overridden to prefer either target or source by specifying the option for the ort algorithm.





git rebase
git rebase <branch> rebases the current branch onto <branch>, where <branch> is typically the main branch.
It finds the common base of the given commit and the HEAD, and creates a linear commit history by appending/replaying all the commits between the base and the HEAD after the given commit:
	  A---B---C (HEAD - feature)
	 /
D---E---F---G (<branch>)

			  A'--B'--C' (HEAD - feature)
			 /
D---E---F---G (<branch>)

These replayed commits are different from the original commits because they contain a different hash for their parent commits.
Replaying the commits involves merging two working trees, just like git merge, so you can specify the same set of strategies for git rebase. But in git rebase this can get really inconvenient because you could get a conflict in each of such commits appended.
Another way to think about git rebase is that it is just git cherry-pick but repeated for all the original commits to be replayed.
git revert
How git cherry-pick and revert use 3-way merge (jvns.ca)
git revert <commit> creates a new commit that reverts the changes brought by the commit <commit> from its parent commit.
To revert commit Y, git uses the three-way merge algorithm with Y as the base, Z as the target and X as the source, so that the changes from the base Y to the source X gets brought into the target Z:
X---Y---Z (HEAD - main)

X---Y---Z---Y' (HEAD - main)

git cherry-pick
How git cherry-pick and revert use 3-way merge (jvns.ca)
git cherry-pick <commit> creates a new commit that brings the same changes brought by the commit <commit> from its parent commit.
To cherry-pick commit Y onto the current branch, git uses the three-way merge algorithm with X as the base, Y as the source and B as the target, so that the changes from the base X to the source Y gets brought into the target B:
A---B (HEAD - main)
 \
  X---Y---Z (feature)

A---B---Y' (HEAD - main)
 \
  X---Y---Z (feature)

git stash
git stashの仕組みが面白い - 鯨飲馬食 (hatenablog.com)
This creates a temporary commit objects W and I for stashing the WIP working tree and the index. The commit W is for storing the working tree, and I for storing the index.
X---Y (HEAD - feature)

    (HEAD - feature)
X---Y---W (WIP on feature)
     \ /
      I (Index on feature)

When you git stash pop or git stash apply, git performs the following steps (or equivalent):

git reset --hard the current branch to W.
git reset --mixed the current branch to Y, keeping the working tree of W.
git reset --mixed the current branch to I.
git reset --soft the current branch to Y, keeping the index of I.

The reference to W is stored as text file under .git/refs/stash. I is simply found by following the parent pointer of W. When there are multiple such WIP commits W, git checks the reflog of .git/refs/stash, which contains the history of the contents of the ref stash.

Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Maintain.title: Git Maintainance
Engineering/Version-Control/Git-Maintain.content:
Git Maintenance
Git - git-maintenance Documentation (git-scm.com)
Run git maintaince inside your most frequently-accessed repositories to run cron jobs to optimise git repository data such as:

Prefetch

Fetch git objects from remotes.
Does not update refs automatically, but once you update them it will be instantaneous.


Garbage collection

Find unreferenced git objects and remove them.



Note that git maintainance is per repository.

Engineering/Version-Control/Golden-Rule-.title: Golden Rule of Rebasing
Engineering/Version-Control/Golden-Rule-.content:
Golden Rule of Rebasing
The Golden Rule of Rebasing in Git | Axosoft (gitkraken.com)


                  
                  Quote
                  
                
The Golden Rule of Rebasing reads: “Never rebase while you’re on a public branch.”

Or rather, “never rewrite history of a public branch”.
The reason is pretty simple.
Imagine you have a feature branch which is 1 commit behind the main branch:
A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	 \
	  X (feature, origin/feature)

Now we decide to ignore the Golden Rule and rebase feature branch on the latest main + force-push it:
A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	  	 \
	   	  X' (feature, origin/feature)

But since feature is a public branch, someone else could have made a new commit Y on it before the rebase + force-push:
A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	 \   \
	  \   X' (origin/feature)
	   \
		X---Y (feature)

At this point they cannot really rebase nor merge, because in both cases the two almost identical commits X' and X would remain in the commit graph … (*)


                  
                  Note
                  
                
If we rebase feature branch on origin/feature, we could squash X' and X later, but we will need to force-push again.

If we had merged instead, it would have looked like this for them:
A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	 \   \
	  X---M (origin/feature)
	   \
		Y (feature)

which they can easily resolve by rebasing feature branch on origin/feature i.e. git pull --rebase.
Similarly for git commit --amend.

Using git push --force-with-lease will prevent you from force-pushing if more commits have been added to the branch:
git push —force-with-lease vs. —force - Stack Overflow
This still will not help in our case because the new commit Y had not been pushed to feature branch when we rebased it, but this is arguably the safer option than git push --force, because once the commit Y is pushed the chaotic scenario described in (*) will occur to everyone who has pulled the latest feature branch`.

Engineering/Version-Control/Ours-and-The.title: Ours and Theirs
Engineering/Version-Control/Ours-and-The.content:
Ours and Theirs
git - ours vs theirs (nitaym.github.io)


                  
                  Warning
                  
                
This page talks about the ours and theirs in git checkout, used during merge conflicts.
Do not confuse this with ours strategy in git merge or git rebase, nor ours and theirs options in git merge’s ort strategy.

When a merge fails (and only when a merge fails really), you can run  git checkout --ours <file> and git checkout --theirs <file> to pick <file> from either source or target branch before merge.
For instance:
$ git checkout master  
$ git merge feature  
Auto-merging Document  
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in README.md
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.
To select the changes done in master:
git checkout --ours README.md
To select the changes done in feature:
git checkout --theirs README.md

Engineering/Version-Control/Rebasing-Pus.title: Rebasing Pushed Branches
Engineering/Version-Control/Rebasing-Pus.content:
Rebasing Pushed Branches
「push済のブランチだけどリベースしたい！」というときの対処方法 (zenn.dev)
Image we have pushed a feature branch but still want to rebase it, while avoiding the worst-case scenario described in Golden Rule of Rebasing:
A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	 \
	  X---Y---Z (feature, origin/feature)

Then we should follow these steps:

Create a new branch e.g. feature-new branch from the feature branch.

A---B---C (main, origin/main)
	 \
	  X---Y---Z (feature, feature-new, origin/feature)


Push the feature-new branch.

A---B---C (main, origin/main)
     \   \
	  \   X'--Y'--Z' (feature-new, origin/feature-new)
	   \
	    X---Y---Z (feature, origin/feature)


Finally delete the feature branch from remote.

A---B---C (main, origin/main)
         \
	      X'--Y'--Z' (feature-new, origin/feature-new)

and this would be no problem if someone else had pushed a new commit to origin/feature.
Optionally, we can squash commits X, Y and Z if we expect conflicts to occur when rebasing.

Engineering/Version-Control/Traversing-P.title: Traversing Parent Commits
Engineering/Version-Control/Traversing-P.content:
Traversing Commit
What’s the difference between HEAD^ and HEAD~ in Git? - Stack Overflow

~2 means up two levels in the hierarchy

Via the first parent if a commit has more than one parent




^2 means the second parent where a commit has more than one parent

G   H   I   J
 \ /     \ /
  D   E   F
   \  |  / \
    \ | /   |
     \|/    |
      B     C
       \   /
        \ /
         A

A =      = A^0
B = A^   = A^1     = A~1
C = A^2
D = A^^  = A^1^1   = A~2
E = B^2  = A^^2
F = B^3  = A^^3
G = A^^^ = A^1^1^1 = A~3
H = D^2  = B^^2    = A^^^2  = A~2^2
I = F^   = B^3^    = A^^3^
J = F^2  = B^3^2   = A^^3^2

Engineering/Version-Control/Upstream-and.title: Upstream and Remote-Tracking Branches
Engineering/Version-Control/Upstream-and.content:
Upstream and Remote-Tracking Branches
Upstream branches are the branches on the remote repository e.g. main
Remote tracking branches are the branches on the local repository which mirrors their corresponding upstream branches e.g. origin/main.

Home.title: Home
Home.content:
Hi there, I’m Taichi Maeda 👋
I’m a third year student at Imperial College London 🎓.
My interests lie in the theory of computation, including topics like programming languages and compilers.
I’m currently based in London 🇬🇧 for my studies, but will be working in Tokyo 🇯🇵 as an intern this summer and (hopefully) after graduation too!
This is my personal Obsidian vault, where I note down interesting discoveries and learnings. Please note that the content reflects my interpretations, so some surprises and mistakes are possible. If you happen to come across any, feel free to let me know by leaving a comment!
Contents
Many of the notes I’ve written are under review to ensure accuracy and conciseness, so there are only a handful notes are made publicly available at the moment:

Computer Science

Algorithms
Networks
Operating Systems
Theory of Computation


Engineering
Languages
Mathematics
Projects

Over the Wire
Type Challenges
Rustonomicon



Recent Projects
Here are some of my recent and ongoing projects:
Markpilot
taichimaeda/markpilot (github.com)
An Obsidian plugin that provides GitHub-flavoured inline completions and chat UI powered by OpenAI APi.
Similar to Obsidian Companion and Obsidian Copilot combined.
Wireplus
taichimaeda/wireplus: Wire Supercharged with Graphviz Vizualisation (github.com)
taichimaeda/wireplus-extension (github.com)
A CLI and VSCode extension that visualises dependency graph generated by wire, a compile-time dependency injection library in Go.
Speed Reader
speed-reader.doc.ic.ac.uk/
An adaptive speed-reading platform that makes use of WebGazer.js for eye-tracking features.
